in_scope_eips: EIP-7823: Set upper bounds for MODEXP
EIP-7934: RLP Execution Block Size Limit
EIP-7951: Precompile for secp256r1 Curve Support
scope_notes: Only Fusaka-specific code is in scope. Any vulnerabilities not specifically related to the upgrade should be submitted via the Ethereum Foundation's Bug Bounty Program.
High-effort DoS (sustained, CPU or bandwidth intensive, requires more than 1 packet or on-chain transaction) is out-of-scope.
Vulnerabilities requiring publicly exposed APIs (JSON-RPC, Beacon API) are explicitly out-of-scope.
All submissions except informational must include an executable coded PoC at time of submission.
Severity is measured by finding's impact on the entire Ethereum network.

actors.name: Transaction Sender (EOA)
actors.description: External account that signs and submits transactions to the network. Controls transaction payload, nonce, gas parameters, and signature values. Can be any network participant.

actors.name: EVM Execution Engine
actors.description: The Ethereum Virtual Machine that executes transaction logic, manages state transitions, and enforces invariants. This is the core trusted component that applies state changes.

actors.name: State Manager
actors.description: The state management subsystem responsible for account storage in tries, snapshot management for transaction isolation, and state root computation. Operates within the EVM's trust domain.

actors.name: Transaction Validator
actors.description: The transaction validation layer that checks intrinsic gas costs, nonce bounds, initcode size limits, and gas limit caps before a transaction enters execution. Acts as a gatekeeper between untrusted input and the EVM.

actors.name: P2P Network Peer
actors.description: A remote node on the P2P network that propagates transactions and blocks. Unauthenticated and can send arbitrary data including malformed transactions.

actors.name: Block Builder / Proposer
actors.description: Entity that constructs blocks, selects transactions from the mempool, and proposes them for inclusion. Has discretion over transaction ordering within a block.

actors.name: Consensus Layer (CL)
actors.description: The beacon chain consensus layer that communicates with the execution layer via Engine API. Authenticated via JWT but the EL-CL boundary still represents a semi-trusted interface.

actors.name: Smart Contract
actors.description: Deployed contract code that can read/write storage, transfer ether, and interact with other contracts. Can invoke state operations (get_storage, set_storage, set_code, etc.) during execution.

actors.name: EIP-7702 Code Delegation Authorizer
actors.description: An EOA that signs an authorization tuple to delegate its code execution to another address via SetCodeTransaction (EIP-7702). This introduces a new trust relationship where an EOA trusts delegated code.

trust_boundaries.description: Transaction submitted by an external sender enters the validation layer. All transaction fields (nonce, gas, data, value, signature) cross this boundary and must be validated before execution.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Transaction payload (nonce, gas_price/max_fee, gas_limit, to, value, data, signature r/s/v, access_list, blob fields, authorization tuples)

trust_boundaries.description: Validated transaction passed from the validation layer to the EVM for execution. At this point intrinsic gas, nonce, initcode, and gas limit checks have passed. The EVM trusts that validation was correctly performed.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Validated transaction object with intrinsic_gas and calldata_floor computed

trust_boundaries.description: EVM invokes state management operations (get_account, set_account, get_storage, set_storage, move_ether, etc.) during transaction execution. The state manager trusts the EVM to invoke operations in the correct order within begin/commit/rollback transaction lifecycle.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Account addresses, storage keys, values (U256), ether amounts, code bytes

trust_boundaries.description: Transactions received from P2P peers enter the transaction decoding and validation pipeline. Peers are unauthenticated and can send arbitrary byte sequences that must be decoded (decode_transaction) and validated before processing.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Raw transaction bytes (RLP-encoded with type prefix), potentially malformed or adversarial

trust_boundaries.description: Smart contract execution triggers state reads and writes (SLOAD/SSTORE, balance transfers, SELFDESTRUCT via destroy_account, transient storage via TLOAD/TSTORE). Contract-controlled addresses and storage keys are used to access the state trie.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Storage keys (Bytes32), storage values (U256), account addresses (Address), code bytes, ether amounts

trust_boundaries.description: Consensus layer communicates with the execution layer via Engine API to trigger block processing, which initiates transaction execution and state transitions. The CL-EL boundary is authenticated via JWT but data integrity depends on both sides.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Block payloads, execution results, state roots

trust_boundaries.description: Block builder selects and orders transactions for inclusion in a block. Transaction ordering affects state outcomes (front-running, MEV). The builder controls which transactions are included and in what order.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Ordered list of transactions, block parameters (gas limit, base fee)

trust_boundaries.description: EIP-7702 SetCodeTransaction allows an EOA to delegate code execution. The authorization tuple (chain_id, address, nonce, signature) crosses the trust boundary and, if valid, results in the EOA's code field being set to a delegation designator. This fundamentally changes the trust model for the authorizing EOA.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Authorization tuples containing chain_id, delegated code address, nonce, and ECDSA signature

trust_boundaries.description: Transaction sender's signature is recovered via secp256k1 to derive the sender address. The recovered address is then used for all state operations (balance deduction, nonce increment). A failure in signature recovery could lead to state operations on wrong accounts.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: ECDSA signature (r, s, v/y_parity), signing hash derived from transaction fields

trust_boundaries.description: After block execution, state_root and storage_root are computed and returned to the consensus layer for finalization. The state root computation requires all snapshots to be empty (no active transactions), creating a temporal constraint.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: State root (Hash32), storage roots (Hash32), execution results

assumptions.text: Transaction signatures (ECDSA secp256k1) are unforgeable. The sender recovery process (recover_sender) correctly derives the sender address from the signature, and no one can produce a valid signature for an address they do not control.

The state trie implementation (trie_get, trie_set, copy_trie, root) is correct and provides faithful snapshots. Deep copies during begin_transaction truly isolate snapshot state from ongoing mutations, ensuring rollback_transaction can restore exact pre-transaction state.

Transaction validation checks (intrinsic gas, nonce overflow, initcode size, gas limit cap per EIP-7825) are exhaustive and correctly enforce all specified constraints before execution begins. No validation bypass allows an invalid transaction to reach the EVM.

The transaction decode/encode functions are bijective for all valid transaction types. decode_transaction(encode_transaction(tx)) == tx for all valid tx. Malformed or adversarial byte sequences are rejected with TransactionTypeError rather than silently misinterpreted.

The snapshot mechanism (begin_transaction / commit_transaction / rollback_transaction) correctly implements nested transaction isolation. Snapshot count invariants (increment by 1 on begin, decrement by 1 on commit/rollback) are always maintained. created_accounts is only cleared when the outermost snapshot is popped.

Ether conservation is maintained across all state transitions. move_ether ensures sender.balance >= amount before transferring, and the total ether supply is invariant (sender decrease == recipient increase). No path exists to create or destroy ether outside of protocol-defined mechanisms.

Empty account cleanup (modify_state destroying accounts with zero nonce, empty code, and zero balance) is correctly triggered after every state modification. No race condition or ordering issue can leave ghost accounts in the state trie.

State root and storage root computations can only occur when no active transaction snapshots exist (assert not state._snapshots). This invariant ensures root computation reflects a fully committed state. Violating this assertion during block production would produce an incorrect state root.

The EIP-7623 calldata cost floor mechanism correctly calculates both intrinsic_gas and calldata_floor, and validation enforces max(intrinsic_gas, calldata_floor) <= tx.gas. This prevents calldata-heavy transactions from underpaying for their resource consumption.

The EIP-7825 transaction gas limit cap (TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT = 16,777,216) is enforced during validation. No transaction exceeding this limit can enter execution, preventing single transactions from consuming excessive block gas.

EIP-7702 authorization tuples are correctly validated: the authorizer's signature covers the chain_id, delegated address, and nonce, preventing cross-chain replay and unauthorized code delegation. The authorization nonce prevents replay of old authorizations.

Signing hash functions for each transaction type (pre-155, EIP-155, EIP-2930, EIP-1559, EIP-4844, EIP-7702) produce unique, non-colliding hashes. The type prefix byte in typed transactions prevents cross-type signature reuse.

Transient storage (EIP-1153) is correctly scoped to a single transaction and follows the same snapshot/rollback semantics as persistent storage. Transient storage is never persisted across transactions and cannot leak between transactions.

The get_storage_original function correctly reads from the outermost snapshot (index 0) for EIP-2200 SSTORE gas accounting. For accounts marked as created within the transaction, it always returns U256(0). An incorrect original value would cause wrong gas refund calculations.

P2P peers cannot cause state corruption by sending malformed transactions. The decode_transaction function must handle all adversarial inputs gracefully, either correctly decoding or raising TransactionTypeError. No RLP decoding failure should cause a crash or undefined behavior.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could attempt to spoof the sender identity by forging an ECDSA signature. If the r/s range validation (0 < r < SECP256K1N, 0 < s <= SECP256K1N/2) or the recovery process has a flaw, an attacker could craft a transaction that recovers to a victim's address, allowing unauthorized ether transfers or nonce increments.
stride_analysis.mitigation: recover_sender validates r and s ranges, enforces low-s normalization (EIP-2), and uses secp256k1_recover with the correct signing hash per transaction type. Each transaction type has a unique signing hash function preventing cross-type signature reuse.

stride_analysis.description: Pre-EIP-155 legacy transactions lack chain_id in the signing hash, enabling cross-chain replay attacks. A transaction signed for one chain can be replayed on another chain, causing the sender to lose funds on the target chain.
stride_analysis.mitigation: EIP-155 transactions include chain_id in the signing hash. Pre-155 transactions are only supported for backward compatibility. Modern clients and wallets default to EIP-155 signing. However, the spec still supports pre-155 signing, leaving a residual risk for users who sign pre-155 transactions.

stride_analysis.description: If the snapshot deep copy mechanism (copy_trie) in begin_transaction is shallow rather than deep, mutations during transaction execution could corrupt the snapshot. When rollback_transaction attempts to restore state, it would restore already-mutated data, violating the atomicity guarantee of transactions.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-003 states snapshots preserve deep copies (mutations do not affect snapshots). The copy_trie function must perform a full deep copy of the trie data structure. Implementation must be verified against this invariant.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious contract could attempt to manipulate storage in a way that causes state trie corruption. For example, if set_storage does not correctly handle the edge case where the account does not exist (INV-017 requires account existence via assertion), an attacker could write storage for nonexistent accounts, leading to orphaned storage tries.
stride_analysis.mitigation: set_storage includes 'assert trie_get(state._main_trie, address) is not None' as its first operation, ensuring the account must exist before storage can be written. This assertion prevents storage writes to nonexistent accounts.

stride_analysis.description: The modify_state function automatically destroys accounts that become empty (zero nonce, empty code, zero balance) after modification. If this cleanup logic is incorrect -- for example, if the empty check evaluates before the modification is fully applied, or if concurrent modifications interfere -- an attacker could create accounts that should be destroyed but persist, or vice versa.
stride_analysis.mitigation: modify_state first applies the modification via set_account, then checks if the resulting account satisfies empty conditions. The sequence is: modify -> check -> destroy. INV-028 and INV-029 document this behavior. Frozen dataclass modify pattern ensures atomic field updates.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could craft a transaction with specific gas values that exploit the interaction between intrinsic_gas and calldata_floor (EIP-7623). If the max(intrinsic_gas, calldata_floor) check has an off-by-one error or the token counting (zero bytes = 1 token, non-zero = 4 tokens) is incorrect, a transaction could pass validation with insufficient gas, potentially causing execution failures or resource exhaustion.
stride_analysis.mitigation: validate_transaction explicitly checks max(intrinsic_gas, calldata_floor) > tx.gas and raises InsufficientTransactionGasError. The token counting algorithm is deterministic based on byte values. EIP-7623 invariants are documented (INV-001 through INV-004 in calculate_intrinsic_cost).

stride_analysis.description: The move_ether function checks sender.balance < amount before transferring. If an implementation performs the balance check but does not atomically apply both the deduction and increment, a race condition or re-entrancy could allow double-spending or ether creation. The spec uses separate modify_state calls for sender and recipient.
stride_analysis.mitigation: move_ether uses a sequential flow: check balance -> modify_state(reduce_sender) -> modify_state(increase_recipient). INV-030 mandates total ether conservation (sender decrease == recipient increase). INV-031 mandates sufficient balance or AssertionError. The frozen dataclass pattern prevents partial updates.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious P2P peer could send a crafted byte sequence where tx[0] is a valid type prefix (1-4) but the remaining bytes (tx[1:]) contain malformed RLP data. If rlp.decode_to does not properly validate all fields of the decoded transaction type, the resulting transaction object could contain inconsistent or out-of-range values.
stride_analysis.mitigation: decode_transaction dispatches based on the type prefix byte and uses rlp.decode_to with specific target types (AccessListTransaction, FeeMarketTransaction, etc.). Unknown type prefixes raise TransactionTypeError. RLP decoding is expected to validate field types and ranges per the RLP specification.

stride_analysis.description: A transaction sender could attempt to deny having sent a transaction. However, the ECDSA signature binds the sender to the transaction content. The sender's address is deterministically derived from the signature via recover_sender, and the transaction is included in the block's transaction trie, which is part of the block hash committed to the consensus layer.
stride_analysis.mitigation: ECDSA signatures provide non-repudiation. The signing hash includes all transaction fields (nonce, gas, to, value, data, and type-specific fields). The transaction hash (get_transaction_hash) provides a unique identifier. Block inclusion provides a permanent record.

stride_analysis.description: The get_storage_original function reads from the outermost snapshot (state._snapshots[0]) to retrieve the original storage value before any modifications in the current transaction. If the snapshot mechanism leaks information about other transactions' state changes (e.g., through shared references instead of deep copies), one transaction could observe another's uncommitted writes.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-039 states original value is read from the first (outermost) snapshot, not current state. INV-003 states snapshots preserve deep copies. The snapshot isolation mechanism, if correctly implemented with deep copies, prevents cross-transaction information leakage.

stride_analysis.description: The account_has_storage function (SG-017) only checks whether an address key exists in state._storage_tries, without checking if the trie contains entries. This could reveal that an account once had storage even if all storage slots were cleared, since empty trie cleanup (INV-018 in set_storage) may not cover all edge cases.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-026 documents this behavior explicitly. set_storage (SG-012) includes cleanup logic: if trie._data == {} after a set operation, the storage trie is deleted from state._storage_tries. This should prevent stale entries, but the invariant acknowledges the limitation.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious P2P peer could flood the network with transactions that have valid type prefixes but deliberately complex or deeply nested RLP structures, causing excessive CPU consumption during rlp.decode_to processing. While each individual decode may be fast, sustained flooding could degrade node performance.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Transaction validation (validate_transaction) enforces gas limits (EIP-7825 TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT), initcode size limits, and nonce bounds. Nodes typically implement rate limiting on P2P connections. However, the decode step itself occurs before validation.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious contract could repeatedly write and then zero out storage slots across many addresses, forcing the state manager to continuously create and destroy storage tries (set_storage creates a new Trie when trie is None, and deletes it when trie._data == {} after set). This trie churn could cause excessive memory allocation and garbage collection pressure.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Gas costs for SSTORE operations (EIP-2200, EIP-3529) are designed to make this economically expensive. Storage trie cleanup (INV-018) prevents unbounded trie accumulation. The EIP-7825 gas limit cap further bounds the operations per transaction.

stride_analysis.description: The close_state function (SG-001) deletes state references in sequence: _main_trie, _storage_tries, _snapshots, created_accounts. If any deletion fails or throws an exception midway, partial state could remain, potentially causing memory leaks in long-running nodes.
stride_analysis.mitigation: close_state follows a strict sequential deletion order (INV-001). In Python implementations, the del operator on instance attributes is generally reliable. However, if the trie implementation holds external resources (file handles, database connections), partial cleanup could leave resources open.

stride_analysis.description: If the commit_transaction or rollback_transaction logic has an off-by-one error in snapshot popping, an attacker could manipulate the snapshot stack to skip a rollback, effectively committing state changes that should have been reverted. This could allow a failed sub-call's state changes to persist.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-004 and INV-006 document the snapshot count invariants. commit_transaction pops one snapshot and conditionally clears created_accounts if it was the last one. rollback_transaction restores state from the popped snapshot. Both also handle transient_storage._snapshots in parallel.

stride_analysis.description: EIP-7702 SetCodeTransaction allows an EOA to set its code to a delegation designator, effectively granting another contract's code execution permissions over the EOA's state (storage, balance). If the authorization validation is flawed -- for example, if the nonce check allows replaying old authorizations -- an attacker could re-delegate an EOA's code after the owner intended to revoke it.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The authorization tuple includes the authorizer's nonce, which must match the current account nonce. The signing hash (signing_hash_7702) binds the authorization to a specific chain_id and nonce, preventing cross-chain replay and stale authorization replay. INV-001 and INV-002 for SG-011 confirm the type prefix and authorization binding.

stride_analysis.description: If the EIP-7825 TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT validation has a bypass (e.g., the check uses > instead of >=, or the constant value is wrong), a transaction could specify an excessively high gas limit. This could allow a single transaction to consume an entire block's gas, starving other transactions and potentially causing consensus failures if different clients implement the check differently.
stride_analysis.mitigation: validate_transaction checks tx.gas > TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT (16,777,216) and raises TransactionGasLimitExceededError. INV-004 documents this constraint. The constant is specified by EIP-7825.

stride_analysis.description: The destroy_account function (SG-008) calls destroy_storage before set_account(address, None). If destroy_storage has a bug in its double-deletion logic (SG-009 shows del state._storage_tries[address] appearing twice in the edge list), and this causes an exception on the second deletion attempt, the account could be left in an inconsistent state: storage destroyed but account still present, or vice versa.
stride_analysis.mitigation: destroy_storage (SG-009) first checks 'address in state._storage_tries' before deletion. The second 'del' in the edge list appears to handle a specific path in the state machine. If the address is not in _storage_tries, the function transitions directly to q_final without any deletion. INV-012 states the storage trie is removed if it existed.

stride_analysis.description: The transient storage snapshot mechanism runs in parallel with persistent storage snapshots but uses separate data structures (transient_storage._snapshots vs state._snapshots). If the two snapshot stacks become desynchronized -- for example, if a commit pops from state._snapshots but fails to pop from transient_storage._snapshots -- the transient storage would be in a different transaction nesting level than persistent storage, leading to inconsistent rollback behavior.
stride_analysis.mitigation: commit_transaction and rollback_transaction both handle transient_storage._snapshots.pop() as a final step after processing state._snapshots. However, the sequential nature means a failure in state snapshot popping could prevent transient snapshot popping. Both INV-004/INV-006 and INV-007 document these parallel operations.

stride_analysis.description: The EIP-2681 nonce overflow check (U256(tx.nonce) >= U256(U64.MAX_VALUE)) prevents nonce from exceeding U64 max. If this check is bypassed or if the comparison uses the wrong type width, a transaction with nonce at or near U64.MAX_VALUE could cause arithmetic overflow in nonce increment operations, leading to nonce wrap-around (0) and enabling replay of historical transactions.
stride_analysis.mitigation: validate_transaction explicitly checks U256(tx.nonce) >= U256(U64.MAX_VALUE) and raises NonceOverflowError. increment_nonce (SG-022) adds Uint(1) to the nonce using safe unsigned arithmetic. INV-034 and INV-035 document the monotonic increase invariant.

stride_analysis.description: State root computation (state_root, SG-014) uses a get_storage_root callback to incorporate all per-account storage roots. If the callback does not enumerate all accounts that have storage, or if it returns EMPTY_TRIE_ROOT for accounts that actually have storage, the state root would be incorrect. This would cause consensus failures because different clients would compute different state roots for the same block.
stride_analysis.mitigation: state_root (SG-014) defines get_storage_root as a closure that calls storage_root for each address. storage_root (SG-013) correctly checks 'address in state._storage_tries' and returns EMPTY_TRIE_ROOT only if no storage trie exists. Both functions assert 'not state._snapshots' to ensure no active transactions. INV-022 and INV-023 document these constraints.

stride_analysis.description: The set_account function (SG-007) directly sets an account in the main trie via trie_set. INV-010 states that setting account to None deletes the account but not its storage. If a caller can invoke set_account(address, None) independently of destroy_account, storage becomes orphaned. A subsequent account creation at the same address could inherit orphaned storage, effectively gaining access to state that belonged to a previously destroyed account.
stride_analysis.mitigation: destroy_account (SG-008) correctly calls destroy_storage before set_account(address, None), ensuring both account and storage are removed together. The set_account function is an internal primitive not directly accessible from contract execution. SELFDESTRUCT (EIP-6780) restrictions limit when accounts can be destroyed.

stride_analysis.description: The mark_account_created function (SG-010) adds an address to state.created_accounts. INV-013 states this marker persists even if account creation reverts. INV-014 notes it is used by get_storage_original and EIP-6780 SELFDESTRUCT constraints. If the created_accounts set is not properly managed across nested transactions (not cleared on rollback but cleared on final commit per INV-005), a contract could manipulate whether an account is considered 'created in this transaction' by forcing specific revert patterns.
stride_analysis.mitigation: commit_transaction clears created_accounts only when len(state._snapshots) == 0 (outermost transaction). rollback_transaction does not explicitly undo mark_account_created additions. This design is intentional per INV-013 (marker persists across reverts). However, this means a reverted account creation still affects get_storage_original behavior, which is the intended semantics for EIP-2200 gas accounting.

stride_analysis.description: A block builder controls transaction ordering. By carefully ordering transactions, a builder can front-run user transactions, sandwich attack DEX trades, or manipulate state in ways that benefit the builder at users' expense. While MEV extraction is a known issue, Fusaka-specific changes (EIP-7623 calldata floor, EIP-7825 gas limit cap) alter the economics of block building and could create new exploitation vectors.
stride_analysis.mitigation: MEV mitigation is an active area of research (PBS, MEV-burn, encrypted mempools). EIP-7623 calldata floor increases the cost of calldata-heavy transactions, potentially affecting MEV bundle construction. EIP-7825 gas limit cap bounds the maximum gas per transaction, limiting single-transaction MEV opportunities.

stride_analysis.description: The signing hash functions for different transaction types use unique type prefix bytes (0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04) to prevent cross-type signature confusion. However, if a new transaction type were introduced with an overlapping signing hash structure, or if the legacy (no-prefix) signing hash could collide with a typed signing hash, an attacker could reuse a signature from one transaction type as another, potentially changing the transaction semantics while preserving the sender identity.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Each typed transaction has a unique prefix byte prepended to the RLP-encoded fields before hashing (signing_hash_2930 uses 0x01, signing_hash_1559 uses 0x02, etc.). Legacy transactions use raw RLP encoding without prefix. The RLP structure itself differs between types, making hash collisions computationally infeasible. INV-002 across all signing hash subgraphs confirms type prefix uniqueness.
